umm-atiyyah: Umm Atiyyah al-Ansariyyah, may Allah be pleased with her, was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad who participated in several military expeditions, tending to the wounded and ill. She was known for her close relationship with the Prophet's household and narrated numerous hadiths concerning women's religious practices, including rulings on washing the deceased, funeral rites, and restrictions on mourning. Her narrations are preserved in the major hadith collections and remain a primary reference for Islamic jurisprudence related to women.
hakim-b-hizam: Hakim ibn Hizam, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad and a nephew of Khadijah, the Prophet's first wife. Born inside the Kaaba, he embraced Islam at the conquest of Mecca and lived to nearly one hundred years of age. He was renowned for his generosity, his extensive business dealings conducted with scrupulous honesty, and his narrations on commercial ethics that are widely cited in books of fiqh.
amr-b-shuaib: Amr ibn Shu'aib was a prominent Tabi'i scholar from Medina and the grandson of the companion Abdullah ibn Amr ibn al-As, may Allah be pleased with them. He transmitted many hadiths through the chain known as Amr ibn Shu'aib, from his father, from his grandfather — a well-known and debated isnad among hadith scholars. He was a reliable narrator whose reports on legal matters, particularly in commerce and family law, are frequently cited in the four major schools of jurisprudence.
ash-shabi: Amir ibn Sharahil al-Sha'bi was one of the greatest Tabi'i scholars of the early Islamic period, known for his encyclopedic knowledge and sharp wit. Born around 19 AH in Kufa, he met many companions of the Prophet and transmitted from them directly, making him a prolific and highly reliable link in many hadith chains. He was a leading jurist of his era, respected by scholars for his broad learning in fiqh, history, and the Quran.
muhammad: Muhammad ibn al-Munkadir was a distinguished Tabi'i scholar and one of the most trusted hadith transmitters in Medina. A son of the companion al-Munkadir, he narrated from numerous companions including Jabir ibn Abdullah, Aisha, and Abu Hurairah, may Allah be pleased with them all. He was celebrated for his piety, asceticism, and the authenticity of his narrations, which appear in all the major hadith collections.
zainab-bint-abi-salama: Zainab bint Abi Salama, may Allah be pleased with her, was a stepdaughter of the Prophet Muhammad through his marriage to Umm Salama. Raised in the Prophet's household, she had direct access to his teachings and the practices of his wives. She narrated hadiths on matters of household conduct, mourning, and women's worship, and her narrations are regarded as highly authentic given her proximity to the Prophet's family.
ismail: Isma'il ibn Abi Khalid was a reliable Tabi'i hadith narrator from Kufa who transmitted from a number of senior companions and their followers. He was praised by leading hadith critics for his precision and trustworthiness, and his reports appear regularly in the Sahihayn and other major collections. He was particularly known for his transmission of narrations related to Islamic practice and belief from the scholars of Kufa.
abu-al-bakhtari: Abu al-Bakhtari, whose name was Sa'id ibn Fayruz al-Ta'i, was a trustworthy Tabi'i scholar from Kufa. He narrated from companions including Ali ibn Abi Talib, Ibn Abbas, and Abu Hurairah, may Allah be pleased with them. Known for his learning and piety, his narrations appear in the major hadith collections, and he was respected by scholars of hadith criticism for the reliability of his transmission.
aisha-and-ibn-abbas: This entry represents narrations transmitted jointly or in parallel by Aisha, may Allah be pleased with her, the beloved wife of the Prophet Muhammad, and Abdullah ibn Abbas, may Allah be pleased with them, the Prophet's cousin and great Quranic scholar. Aisha was the most prolific female narrator of hadith, while Ibn Abbas was renowned for his depth of Quranic interpretation and legal insight. Together their combined corpus covers virtually every domain of Islamic knowledge.
abu-thalaba-al-khushani: Abu Tha'laba al-Khushani, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad from the tribe of Khushain. He pledged allegiance to the Prophet and narrated hadiths on various religious rulings including hunting, eating, and general conduct. His narrations are found in several of the six major hadith collections, and he is considered a reliable witness to the Prophet's teachings and practice.
abu-aiyub-al-ansari: Abu Ayyub al-Ansari, may Allah be pleased with him, was a distinguished companion of the Prophet Muhammad from the Ansar of Medina. He had the great honor of hosting the Prophet in his home upon the Prophet's arrival in Medina during the Hijra. He participated in many military campaigns and continued striving in the path of Allah into old age, dying during the siege of Constantinople, where he was buried. His narrations on prayer, worship, and general practice are widely cited.
imran: Imran ibn Husayn, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad who embraced Islam in the seventh year after the Hijra. He was known for his asceticism, his notable spiritual states, and the angels' greetings reported in his narrations. He settled in Basra, where he became a leading religious authority, and his hadiths on acts of worship, legal rulings, and morality appear in all the major hadith collections.
shaqiq: Shaqiq ibn Salama al-Asadi, also known as Abu Wa'il, was a prominent Tabi'i scholar from Kufa who narrated from many companions of the Prophet including Abdullah ibn Masud, Hudhayfah ibn al-Yaman, and Ali ibn Abi Talib, may Allah be pleased with them. He was held in the highest regard by hadith scholars for his reliability and long chain of transmission. His narrations are found in the Sahihayn and throughout the major hadith literature.
musab-bin-sad: Mus'ab ibn Sa'd was the son of the illustrious companion Sa'd ibn Abi Waqqas, may Allah be pleased with him, one of the ten companions given glad tidings of Paradise. Mus'ab narrated from his father and other companions, transmitting important hadiths on worship, prayer, and the virtues of the companions. He was a trustworthy Tabi'i narrator whose reports appear in the major hadith collections.
warrad: Warrad was the secretary and freed slave of Al-Mughirah ibn Shu'bah, the companion of the Prophet, may Allah be pleased with him. He narrated hadiths on behalf of his master, transmitting reports including the famous supplication recited after the prayer. Hadith scholars regarded him as a reliable transmitter, and his narrations, though limited, appear in the Sahihayn and other major collections.
muhammad-bin-seereen: Muhammad ibn Sirin was one of the foremost Tabi'i scholars of Basra, celebrated equally for his mastery of hadith, his expertise in dream interpretation, and his exceptional piety. He narrated from prominent companions including Abu Hurairah, Anas ibn Malik, and Ibn Umar, may Allah be pleased with them. His strict standards of hadith transmission — he was among the first to insist on scrutinizing the chain of narrators — made him a towering figure in the science of hadith criticism.
jundab: Jundab ibn Abdullah al-Bajali, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad known for his straightforward and austere character. He narrated hadiths on faith, repentance, and sincere worship, including well-known reports warning against ostentation. He later settled in Basra and played a role in early Islamic governance. His narrations are preserved in the major hadith collections.
amr-bin-al-harith: Amr ibn al-Harith, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad and a brother-in-law through the Prophet's wife Juwayriyah. He was known for his modest lifestyle and narrated that the Prophet left no worldly wealth at the time of his death save a white mule, weapons, and a small piece of land dedicated as charity. His narrations are found in the Sahihayn.
ata: Ata ibn Abi Rabah was one of the greatest Tabi'i jurists and hadith scholars of Mecca. Born of Abyssinian origin, he rose to become the foremost religious authority in the holy city, narrating from dozens of companions including Ibn Abbas, Aisha, and Abu Hurairah, may Allah be pleased with them. He was particularly renowned for his expertise in the rites of Hajj and Umrah, and his students included Imam al-Shafi'i's teacher Ibn Jurayj.
muhammad-bin-jubair-bin-mutim: Muhammad ibn Jubayr ibn Mut'im was a Tabi'i scholar and son of the companion Jubayr ibn Mut'im, may Allah be pleased with him. He narrated hadiths from his father and other companions, transmitting reports on topics including the names of the Prophet, matters of lineage, and aspects of worship. He was considered a reliable narrator whose reports appear in the major hadith collections.
abu-ad-darda: Abu al-Darda, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad renowned for his deep wisdom, asceticism, and love of knowledge. He embraced Islam at the time of the Battle of Badr and became one of the foremost scholars among the companions. He settled in Syria and served as a judge and teacher there, narrating many hadiths on worship, the Quran, and spiritual guidance.
abdur-rahman-bin-abu-bakr: Abdur-Rahman ibn Abi Bakr, may Allah be pleased with him, was the son of Abu Bakr al-Siddiq, the first caliph. He embraced Islam and narrated hadiths from the Prophet Muhammad, including narrations related to the People of the Suffa and charitable giving. He was known for his bravery in early Islamic battles and his close connection to the Prophet's household through his family.
abdur-rahman-bin-auf: Abdur-Rahman ibn Awf, may Allah be pleased with him, was one of the ten companions promised Paradise and among the earliest to embrace Islam. A successful merchant, he was known for his extraordinary generosity, once donating half his wealth in charity. He narrated hadiths on prayer, fasting, trade, and governance, and his narrations are preserved across all the major hadith collections.
suwaid-bin-an-numan: Suwaid ibn al-Nu'man, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad from the Ansar of Medina. He participated in the expedition to Khaybar and narrated hadiths about the Prophet's practices during that journey, including how the Prophet handled food provisions. His narrations appear in the Sahihayn and are cited in discussions of Islamic dietary and travel law.
abdur-rahman-bin-abi-laila: Abdur-Rahman ibn Abi Laila was a highly regarded Tabi'i scholar and jurist from Kufa who narrated from many prominent companions including Ali ibn Abi Talib, Bilal, and Ka'b ibn Ujra, may Allah be pleased with them. He served as a judge in Kufa and was considered one of the most learned men of his time in religious law. His narrations are found in the major hadith collections.
ibn-masud: Abdullah ibn Masud, may Allah be pleased with him, was one of the most eminent companions of the Prophet Muhammad and the first to recite the Quran openly in Mecca. He was the Prophet's personal servant for a time and was deeply trusted for his understanding of Quranic recitation and Islamic law. He later settled in Kufa as a teacher and religious authority, and his vast corpus of narrations covers every domain of Islamic practice.
khalid-bin-al-walid: Khalid ibn al-Walid, may Allah be pleased with him, known as the Sword of Allah, was one of the greatest military commanders in Islamic history. He embraced Islam in the eighth year after the Hijra and thereafter led Muslims to remarkable victories in Arabia, Syria, Iraq, and Persia. He narrated hadiths from the Prophet and his narrations on permissible foods, moral conduct, and other matters appear in the major hadith collections.
tariq-bin-shihab: Tariq ibn Shihab al-Ahmasi was a Tabi'i narrator from Kufa who saw the Prophet Muhammad as a child though did not narrate from him directly. He narrated extensively from senior companions including Abu Bakr, Umar, Ali, and Hudhayfah, may Allah be pleased with them, and was regarded as a reliable transmitter by hadith scholars. His narrations appear in the Sahihayn and other major collections.
um-habiba: Umm Habibah, may Allah be pleased with her, whose name was Ramlah bint Abi Sufyan, was a wife of the Prophet Muhammad and daughter of Abu Sufyan. She emigrated to Abyssinia in the early years of Islam and later married the Prophet, who sent for her from Abyssinia. She narrated hadiths on prayer, worship, and the Prophet's conduct, and her narrations are preserved in the major hadith collections.
abdullah-bin-masud-ra: Abdullah ibn Masud, may Allah be pleased with him, was one of the most learned and trusted companions of the Prophet Muhammad. He memorized more than seventy surahs directly from the Prophet's lips and was one of four companions the Prophet commanded his followers to learn the Quran from. His juristic opinions formed the basis of the Hanafi school, and he narrated hundreds of hadiths on all aspects of Islamic practice and belief.
abu-salamah: Abu Salamah ibn Abd al-Rahman ibn Awf was a leading Tabi'i scholar from Medina and the son of the companion Abdur-Rahman ibn Awf, may Allah be pleased with him. He was a frequent transmitter of hadiths from Aisha, Abu Hurairah, and other companions, and his narrations appear throughout the Sahihayn and the Sunan collections. He was regarded as one of the seven leading jurists of Medina.
abdullah-bin-buraidah: Abdullah ibn Buraidah was a Tabi'i scholar from Khurasan and the son of the companion Buraidah ibn al-Husayb, may Allah be pleased with him. He narrated hadiths from his father and other companions, transmitting reports on prayer, fasting, military campaigns, and supplications. He served as a judge in Marw and was considered a reliable narrator by the scholars of hadith.
bahz-bin-hakim: Bahz ibn Hakim was a Tabi'i narrator who transmitted hadiths through the well-known chain from his grandfather Mu'awiyah ibn Haydah, a companion of the Prophet, may Allah be pleased with him. His narrations, while sometimes debated among hadith critics, cover topics including the rights of neighbors, modesty, and charitable giving. His reports appear in the Sunan collections and are cited in books of jurisprudence.
abd-allaah-b-umar: Abdullah ibn Umar, may Allah be pleased with him, was the son of Caliph Umar ibn al-Khattab and one of the most prolific narrators among the companions. Devoted to following the Prophet's example in every detail, he was known for his meticulous piety and his scrupulous adherence to the Sunnah. He narrated approximately 2,630 hadiths covering all aspects of Islamic life, and his narrations are foundational to Islamic jurisprudence.
amr-ibn-al-as: Amr ibn al-As, may Allah be pleased with him, was a brilliant military strategist and companion of the Prophet Muhammad who embraced Islam in the eighth year after the Hijra. He led the Muslim conquest of Egypt and served as its governor. He narrated hadiths from the Prophet on topics including sincere repentance, prayer, and governance, and his narrations are found in the major hadith collections.
abdullah-ibn-mughaffal: Abdullah ibn Mughaffal, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad and one of those who gave the pledge of allegiance under the tree at Hudaybiyyah. He narrated hadiths on prayer, the Quran, and the Prophet's habits and manner of recitation. He settled in Basra after the conquests and became a local religious authority there.
zayd-ibn-khalid-al-juhani: Zayd ibn Khalid al-Juhani, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad who participated in the expedition of Hudaybiyyah. He narrated hadiths on prayer, fasting, financial transactions, and legal rulings including on lost property and testimony. His narrations are found in the Sahihayn and the four Sunan collections.
wathilah-ibn-al-asqa: Wathilah ibn al-Asqa, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad who narrated a number of hadiths on faith, sincerity, and righteous conduct. He lived to a very advanced age, reportedly dying at over one hundred years old in Syria, where he became a known narrator in the later generation of companions. His narrations appear in the major hadith collections.
muawiyah-ibn-abusufyan: Muawiyah ibn Abi Sufyan, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad who served as one of his scribes recording the revelation. He later became the founder of the Umayyad Caliphate and served as caliph for nearly twenty years, during which the Islamic state expanded greatly. He narrated hadiths from the Prophet on worship, governance, and general conduct.
abdullah-ibn-busr: Abdullah ibn Busr, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad who was among the youngest to narrate from him directly. He lived to a great age and was the last companion to die in Syria, allowing later generations to benefit from his direct link to the Prophet. He narrated hadiths on supplication, prayer, fasting, and the merit of various acts of worship.
abd-allah-b-abbas: Abdullah ibn Abbas, may Allah be pleased with him, was the cousin of the Prophet Muhammad and is regarded as the greatest Quranic exegete among the companions. The Prophet supplicated for him specifically, asking Allah to grant him understanding of the religion, and he fulfilled that prayer through his unparalleled scholarship. He narrated thousands of hadiths and his explanations of the Quran form the foundation of the science of tafsir.
abd-allah-b-umar: Abdullah ibn Umar, may Allah be pleased with him, was a devoted companion of the Prophet who embraced Islam as a young man alongside his father Umar ibn al-Khattab. He was deeply committed to emulating the Prophet's Sunnah in every detail and narrated extensively from both the Prophet and from Abu Bakr, his father Umar, and other senior companions. His narrations are among the most frequently cited in Islamic jurisprudence.
yala: Ya'la ibn Umayyah, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad who participated in various military campaigns. He narrated hadiths on the rites of Hajj, dress for pilgrims in the state of ihram, and related matters. His narrations are found in the Sahihayn and other major collections and are cited in discussions of Hajj jurisprudence.
kuraib: Kuraib was a freed slave of Abdullah ibn Abbas, may Allah be pleased with him, and a reliable Tabi'i transmitter who narrated extensively on behalf of his master. He served as a secretary to Ibn Abbas and transmitted many important hadiths from him, including those related to prayer, the beginning of months, and legal rulings. Hadith scholars regarded him as trustworthy and his narrations appear in the major collections.
umm-hani: Umm Hani bint Abi Talib, may Allah be pleased with her, was the daughter of Abu Talib and thus a cousin of the Prophet Muhammad. She is associated with one of the most significant events in Islamic history — the Night Journey and Ascension — as the Prophet is reported to have departed from her home that night. She narrated hadiths on prayer, the Night Journey, and other matters, and her narrations appear in the major hadith collections.
abuthalabah-al-khushani: Abu Tha'labah al-Khushani, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad known for his narrations on permissible and impermissible food, particularly the ruling on hunting with trained animals and dogs. He pledged allegiance to the Prophet and narrated hadiths on various legal and ethical matters that are preserved in the Sahihayn and the Sunan works.
rafi-b-khadij: Rafi ibn Khadij, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad from the Ansar of Medina. He participated in numerous military campaigns and was wounded at the Battle of Uhud. He narrated important hadiths on agricultural contracts, particularly sharecropping arrangements, which shaped Islamic commercial law. His narrations are found in the Sahihayn and the Sunan collections.
usamah-b-zaid: Usamah ibn Zayd, may Allah be pleased with him, was the son of Zayd ibn Harithah, the freed slave and beloved companion of the Prophet, and was himself deeply loved by the Prophet, who called him and his father among his most beloved people. He was appointed commander of a military expedition at a young age. He narrated hadiths on worship, the Prophet's character, and legal rulings, and his narrations appear in the major collections.
al-miqdam-ibn-madikarib: Al-Miqdam ibn Ma'dikarib, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad from Syria. He narrated hadiths on the authority of the Sunnah alongside the Quran, the rights of guests, the prohibition of killing certain animals, and other matters. He lived to an advanced age in Syria and narrated many hadiths that appear in the Sunan works and other major collections.
al-numan-b-bashir: Al-Nu'man ibn Bashir, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad and the first Ansari child born after the Hijra. He narrated the well-known hadith comparing lawful and unlawful matters to clear and doubtful gray areas, which is considered one of the foundational principles of Islamic jurisprudence. He served as governor of Kufa and Homs and narrated many hadiths preserved in the Sahihayn.
muadh-ibn-anas: Mu'adh ibn Anas al-Juhani, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad who narrated hadiths on the merit of patience, supplication, and various acts of worship. He settled in Egypt after the conquests and became a recognized religious authority there. His narrations appear in the Sunan works and other major hadith collections.
aburimthah: AbuRimthah, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad who narrated a description of the Prophet's physical appearance, including details of the Prophet's hair color and build. His account is among those cited in works on the Prophet's physical attributes (shama'il). His narrations appear in the Sunan of al-Tirmidhi and other collections.
abu-huraira-and-abu-said: This entry represents narrations transmitted jointly or in parallel by Abu Hurairah and Abu Sa'id al-Khudri, may Allah be pleased with them both. Abu Hurairah was the most prolific narrator of hadith among all companions, while Abu Sa'id al-Khudri was a major narrator on matters of worship, social ethics, and eschatology. Their combined narrations form a vast corpus covering virtually every dimension of Islamic practice.
aisha-and-abdullah-bin-abbas: This entry represents narrations transmitted jointly or in parallel by Aisha, may Allah be pleased with her, and Abdullah ibn Abbas, may Allah be pleased with him. Aisha was the most knowledgeable of the Prophet's wives and the most prolific female narrator of hadith, while Ibn Abbas was the foremost exegete of the Quran among the companions. Together they represent two of Islam's greatest authorities on religious knowledge.
al-ahnaf-bin-qais: Al-Ahnaf ibn Qais al-Tamimi was a leading Tabi'i figure renowned for his wisdom, forbearance, and eloquence. He lived during the time of the companions and narrated from several of them including Umar, Ali, and Abu Bakr, may Allah be pleased with them. He was regarded as one of the wisest Arabs of his generation and played an important role in early Islamic governance in Basra.
zaid-bin-khalid-and-abu-huraira: This entry represents narrations transmitted jointly or in parallel by Zayd ibn Khalid al-Juhani and Abu Hurairah, may Allah be pleased with them both. Zayd was an Ansari companion who narrated on legal matters including lost property and testimony, while Abu Hurairah was the most prolific narrator of hadith overall. Their combined narrations cover a wide range of legal and ethical topics.
shuba: Shu'bah ibn al-Hajjaj was one of the most eminent hadith scholars of Basra and a pioneer in the systematic criticism of narrators. He was the first scholar to apply rigorous scrutiny to the reliability of transmitters, earning the title Prince of the Believers in Hadith from some scholars. He narrated from hundreds of Tabi'in and his high standards of authentication made his seal of approval a benchmark of reliability in hadith scholarship.
marwan-bin-al-hakam-and-al-miswar-bin-ma: This entry represents narrations transmitted jointly by Marwan ibn al-Hakam and al-Miswar ibn Makhrama, may Allah be pleased with al-Miswar. Al-Miswar was a companion of the Prophet who narrated important hadiths on the Treaty of Hudaybiyyah and related events. Marwan transmitted alongside him in several key narrations concerning the Prophet's era. Their combined accounts are valuable historical records of events from the Prophet's time.
mujashi: Mujashi ibn Masud al-Sulami, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad who participated in the pledges of allegiance to the Prophet. He narrated hadiths on the conditions and significance of the pledge of allegiance (bay'ah) and other matters of Islamic practice. His narrations appear in the Sahihayn.
salim-abu-an-nadr: Salim Abu al-Nadr was a reliable Tabi'i narrator and freed slave of Umar ibn Ubaydullah al-Taymi in Medina. He narrated hadiths from Companions and senior Tabi'in, including the famous narration of the letter of Abdullah ibn Abi Awfa regarding the ruling on fighting during the months of truce. His narrations appear in the Sahihayn and are cited by hadith scholars as trustworthy.
abu-uthman-an-nahdi: Abu Uthman al-Nahdi, whose name was Abd al-Rahman ibn Mall, was a senior Tabi'i scholar who narrated from dozens of companions including Abu Bakr, Umar, Uthman, and Ali, may Allah be pleased with them. He lived to a very advanced age and was one of the most prolific Tabi'i narrators of his era. His narrations appear extensively in the Sahihayn and the Sunan works.
al-bara-and-ibn-abi-aufa: This entry represents narrations transmitted jointly or in parallel by al-Bara ibn Azib and Abdullah ibn Abi Awfa, may Allah be pleased with them both. Al-Bara was a young Ansari companion who participated in fifteen expeditions and narrated widely on the Prophet's practice. Ibn Abi Awfa was among the last companions to die in Kufa. Together their narrations provide important accounts of the Prophet's military campaigns and general religious practice.
ubaidullah-bin-abdullah-bin-utba: Ubaidullah ibn Abdullah ibn Utbah ibn Masud was a leading Tabi'i scholar of Medina and one of the seven great jurists of Medina. He narrated from prominent companions including Aisha, Ibn Abbas, and Abu Hurairah, may Allah be pleased with them, and was a teacher of the renowned Imam al-Zuhri. His narrations appear in the Sahihayn and throughout the major hadith collections.
abdullah-bin-qais: Abdullah ibn Qais, known as Abu Musa al-Ash'ari, may Allah be pleased with him, was a prominent companion of the Prophet Muhammad and a leading scholar of the Quran, hadith, and Islamic law. He served as governor of Basra and Kufa and was renowned for the beauty of his Quranic recitation. He narrated extensively on matters of prayer, fasting, and legal rulings, and his narrations are found across all the major hadith collections.
talha-bin-ubaidullah: Talha ibn Ubaydillah, may Allah be pleased with him, was one of the ten companions given glad tidings of Paradise and among the earliest to embrace Islam. He was celebrated for his extraordinary bravery at the Battle of Uhud, where he shielded the Prophet from arrows with his own body, earning him the title Talha the Generous and Talha the Good. He narrated hadiths on worship and the Prophet's character preserved in the major collections.
asma-bint-abi-bakr: Asma bint Abi Bakr, may Allah be pleased with her, was the daughter of Abu Bakr al-Siddiq and elder sister of Aisha, earning the epithet Dhat al-Nitaqayn (She of the Two Waistbands) for her role in provisioning the Prophet and her father during the Hijra. She lived to nearly one hundred years of age and narrated numerous hadiths on worship, the Prophet's practice, and historical events from the early Islamic period.
samura-bin-jundub: Samura ibn Jundub, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad who embraced Islam at a young age. He narrated hadiths on prayer, dreams, and the Prophet's commands regarding various matters of religious practice. He later served as governor of Basra on several occasions and was known as a narrator of the Prophet's traditions across the major hadith collections.
muhammad-bin-al-munkadir: Muhammad ibn al-Munkadir was a distinguished Tabi'i scholar and one of the most trusted hadith transmitters in Medina. He narrated from prominent companions including Jabir ibn Abdullah, Aisha, and Abu Hurairah, may Allah be pleased with them, and was celebrated for his piety, weeping out of fear of Allah, and the authenticity of his narrations. His reports appear in the Sahihayn and all the major hadith collections.
umar-bin-abi-salama: Umar ibn Abi Salama, may Allah be pleased with him, was a stepson of the Prophet Muhammad through his mother Umm Salama. He grew up in the Prophet's household and narrated hadiths on table manners, the correct way of eating, and the Prophet's daily conduct. His narrations provide intimate glimpses into the Prophet's household life and are preserved in the major hadith collections.
said-bin-al-harith: Sa'id ibn al-Harith al-Ansari was a Tabi'i narrator from Medina who transmitted from Abu Sa'id al-Khudri and other companions of the Prophet, may Allah be pleased with them. He narrated hadiths on prayer including the ruling on reciting the opening chapter al-Fatiha in every unit of prayer. His narrations appear in the major hadith collections and are cited in works of jurisprudence.
itban-bin-malik: Itban ibn Malik, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad from the Ansar of Medina. He had difficulty walking to the mosque due to his condition and the Prophet visited his home and prayed there, establishing a personal prayer place for him. He narrated the famous hadith affirming that sincere profession of faith protects a person from Hellfire, a narration preserved in the Sahihayn.
kab: Ka'b ibn Malik, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad and one of the three companions who remained behind from the expedition of Tabuk. His account of the story of those who stayed behind — and the sincere repentance that followed — is one of the most moving narratives in the hadith literature, preserved in Sahih al-Bukhari. He was also a renowned poet who used his talent in defense of Islam.
aun-bin-abi-juhaifa: Awn ibn Abi Juhayfah was a Tabi'i narrator and the son of the companion Abu Juhayfah, may Allah be pleased with him. He transmitted hadiths from his father and other companions, preserving important narrations including descriptions of the Prophet's physical appearance and his practices in prayer. His narrations appear in the Sahihayn and are cited by scholars in discussions of the Prophet's conduct and worship.
malik-bin-huwairith: Malik ibn al-Huwairith, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad who came to Medina as a young man with a group from his tribe to learn Islam. The Prophet gave him and his companions direct instruction in the performance of prayer, telling them to pray as they had seen him pray. He narrated hadiths on prayer and the Prophet's practice that are preserved in the Sahihayn.
anas-bin-seereen: Anas ibn Sirin was a Tabi'i scholar and the brother of the famous Muhammad ibn Sirin. He narrated from companions including Anas ibn Malik and Ibn Umar, may Allah be pleased with them, and was regarded as a reliable transmitter by hadith scholars. His narrations appear in the major hadith collections, though he is less prolific than his brother.
abu-mamar: Abu Ma'mar, whose name was Abdullah ibn Sakhbarah, was a Tabi'i scholar from Kufa who narrated from companions including Abdullah ibn Masud and others. He was regarded as a reliable narrator by hadith scholars and transmitted narrations on prayer and recitation. His reports appear in the major hadith collections and are cited in discussions of Quranic recitation and Islamic practice.
marwan-bin-al-hakam: Marwan ibn al-Hakam was a Umayyad ruler who served as caliph and narrated hadiths he received from companions of the Prophet, particularly from Zayd ibn Thabit and Uthman ibn Affan, may Allah be pleased with them. While his political career was controversial among early Muslims, his narrations in the hadith collections are evaluated on their individual merits by hadith scholars.
abu-wail: Abu Wa'il, whose name was Shaqiq ibn Salama al-Asadi, was a highly regarded Tabi'i scholar from Kufa. He narrated from many prominent companions including Abdullah ibn Masud, Hudhayfah ibn al-Yaman, and Ali ibn Abi Talib, may Allah be pleased with them. A prolific and trusted transmitter, his narrations appear throughout the Sahihayn and the Sunan collections and he is considered one of the most reliable Tabi'i narrators of Kufa.
